category_summary:
AI research direction led the day. **François Chollet** [argued AI will converge](/?date=2026-07-03&category=social#item-de7ca7ff5f63) toward intuition-guided symbolic world modeling and deep-learning-guided program synthesis, citing ARC-AGI trends, while [affirming LLMs remain essential](/?date=2026-07-03&category=social#item-0db8c0566d3b) for intuition and human-AI communication.

- **Ethan Mollick** drove the agentic-workflow conversation, calling **continual learning** [the biggest barrier to explosive AI adoption](/?date=2026-07-03&category=social#item-1564872f9852) and validating **Anthropic's Mythos/Fable** [cybersecurity capabilities](/?date=2026-07-03&category=social#item-322ce718581b) from hands-on use. He also stressed [building your own benchmarks](/?date=2026-07-03&category=social#item-20357e5e6655) over chasing cheaper models.
- **John Carmack** delivered widely-praised [technical clarifications on GPU utilization](/?date=2026-07-03&category=social#item-c3d8f07a04b9) and [Nsight profiling tools](/?date=2026-07-03&category=social#item-a2e978254cee).
- **Inference optimization** trended as the **vLLM** project [shipped native support](/?date=2026-07-03&category=social#item-f3d39b18c4db) for **DeepSeek's** DSpark semi-autoregressive speculative decoder with concrete speedups.

On strategy and openness, **NVIDIA** [framed AI as shifting](/?date=2026-07-03&category=social#item-d68ff7cfc0d5) from training to always-on token production, demanding new financing models. **Thinking Machines Lab** and **Bridgewater** [showed expert-tuned open-weight models](/?date=2026-07-03&category=social#item-c17a0da26cbb) beating frontier baselines, while **Hugging Face's Thomas Wolf** [urged people to update priors](/?date=2026-07-03&category=social#item-ec1788ae8402) on open-source speech-to-speech.
category_summary_html:
AI research direction led the day. François Chollet argued AI will converge toward intuition-guided symbolic world modeling and deep-learning-guided program synthesis, citing ARC-AGI trends, while affirming LLMs remain essential for intuition and human-AI communication.

  • Ethan Mollick drove the agentic-workflow conversation, calling continual learning the biggest barrier to explosive AI adoption and validating Anthropic's Mythos/Fable cybersecurity capabilities from hands-on use. He also stressed building your own benchmarks over chasing cheaper models.
  • John Carmack delivered widely-praised technical clarifications on GPU utilization and Nsight profiling tools.
  • Inference optimization trended as the vLLM project shipped native support for DeepSeek's DSpark semi-autoregressive speculative decoder with concrete speedups.

On strategy and openness, NVIDIA framed AI as shifting from training to always-on token production, demanding new financing models. Thinking Machines Lab and Bridgewater showed expert-tuned open-weight models beating frontier baselines, while Hugging Face's Thomas Wolf urged people to update priors on open-source speech-to-speech.

items.content: Eventually, much of AI will converge towards intuition-guided symbolic world modeling, i.e. deep learning-guided program synthesis. It is inevitable. Symbolic modeling lets a system construct a compact, reusable, highly generalizable mental model of a problem space using minimal data.

items.title: @davepl1968 @Dell GPU utilization is a measure of the time the GPU is not completely idle, it will n...
items.content: @davepl1968 @Dell GPU utilization is a measure of the time the GPU is not completely idle, it will not go down because of thermal or power throttling. If less than 100%, the GPU is going idle waiting for the CPU to give it more work.
items.author: @ID_AA_Carmack
items.summary: John Carmack clarifies that GPU utilization measures non-idle time and drops only when the GPU waits on the CPU, not from thermal or power throttling.
items.reasoning: High-credibility engineer delivering a precise, widely useful technical clarification with strong engagement (332 likes, 18k views).

items.content:
Continual learning is probably the biggest barrier to explosive AI adoption (& may have big implications for recursive self-improvement as well)

As long as you deal with amnesiac models that require humans to do the learning for them, adoption will be gated by human processes.

items.title: You really need your own benchmarks. If you are translating hieroglyphics, use Gemini 3.5 Flash. If ...
items.content:
You really need your own benchmarks. If you are translating hieroglyphics, use Gemini 3.5 Flash. If you are running a vending machine use Opus 4.8. 

(This is one reason why I am skeptical of just swapping out models to optimize costs or generic benchmarks without testing first)

items.title: Mira Murati's Thinking Machines Lab and Bridgewater, the world's largest hedge fund, published joint...
items.content:
Mira Murati's Thinking Machines Lab and Bridgewater, the world's largest hedge fund, published joint results on using AI for a basic but important task in investing: 

Deciding which news deserves an analyst's attention.

First, Bridgewater tried the frontier models. GPT, Claude, and Gemini variants averaged around 50% across six filtering tests.

Then, expert investors wrote the prompts themselves. Accuracy climbed into the mid-70s. Still shy of the 80% the investors said they'd need before trusting a system in daily work.

Then came the fine-tune using TML's Tinker API, with Bridgewater training an open-weight model on its experts' real judgement calls.

The results: 84.7%,  with mistakes down 29.8% versus the top frontier model tested, and at a 13.8x lower per-task cost.

Murati, on X: "Experts improving AI that empowers experts."
items.author: @TheRundownAI
items.summary: Continuing yesterday's [Social](/?date=2026-07-01&category=social#item-984b11cdc75a) thread on Bridgewater's Tinker fine-tuning, now with the joint results published, The Rundown summarizes joint Thinking Machines Lab and Bridgewater results showing that fine-tuning an open-weight model on expert judgment via the Tinker API reached 84.7 percent accuracy on news-filtering, beating frontier models with fewer errors and far lower cost.
items.reasoning: Concrete, notable result with specific numbers demonstrating expert-in-the-loop fine-tuning beating frontier models, relevant to open-weight and finance-AI themes.

items.content_html:
Mira Murati's Thinking Machines Lab and Bridgewater, the world's largest hedge fund, published joint results on using AI for a basic but important task in investing:

Deciding which news deserves an analyst's attention.

First, Bridgewater tried the frontier models. GPT, Claude, and Gemini variants averaged around 50% across six filtering tests.

Then, expert investors wrote the prompts themselves. Accuracy climbed into the mid-70s. Still shy of the 80% the investors said they'd need before trusting a system in daily work.

Then came the fine-tune using TML's Tinker API, with Bridgewater training an open-weight model on its experts' real judgement calls.

The results: 84.7%, with mistakes down 29.8% versus the top frontier model tested, and at a 13.8x lower per-task cost.

Murati, on X: "Experts improving AI that empowers experts."

items.title:
🚀 @deepseek_ai's DSpark speculative decoding now runs natively in vLLM!

What it is: a semi-autoregr...
items.content:
🚀 @deepseek_ai's DSpark speculative decoding now runs natively in vLLM!

What it is: a semi-autoregressive drafter that proposes several tokens in parallel with non-causal sliding-window attention, then verifies them in a single pass. Output stays identical, decoding takes fewer steps.

How vLLM runs it: it reuses the existing SparseMLA backends instead of custom attention kernels, captures the full draft backbone and sampling loop in one CUDA graph, and works with prefix caching and FP8 KV cache.

Performance on DeepSeek-V4-Pro-DSpark (verified on NVIDIA 8×B300 GPUs):
- ~250 tokens/s at batch size 1
- average acceptance length ~5
- 12-42% higher acceptance than MTP across draft depths

Run with vLLM nightly today:
vllm serve deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-V4-Pro-DSpark -tp 8 --trust-remote-code --kv-cache-dtype fp8 --speculative-config '{"method":"dspark","num_speculative_tokens":7,"draft_sample_method":"greedy"}'

DSpark Core PR: https://t.co/Yyo6rYX4jG

Thanks @deepseek_ai for open-sourcing DSpark, and to @NVIDIAAI and the vLLM community for landing it! 🙏

items.reasoning: High technical depth with concrete performance numbers on a current DeepSeek model, strong engagement and views; a substantive open-source inference advance.

items.title: Does it mean LLMs / LRMs go away? Not at all. In the short term, they are still the best way to perf...
items.content: Does it mean LLMs / LRMs go away? Not at all. In the short term, they are still the best way to perform intuition guidance (codegen). In the long term, even if they become obsolete for reasoning itself, we will still need models of language in order to communicate with AI systems

AI is shifting from model training to always-on token production, and that shift demands a new business model.

NVIDIA is partnering with AI clouds to deploy large‑scale, multi‑tenant AI factories through revenue-sharing and credit-support. This opens up compute access to the fast‑growing AI ecosystem of startups, model builders, enterprises, research organizations and regional AI players.

items.title:
I agree that this is an important interview of @PalantirTech's CEO.

Matches what I've heard from en...
items.content:
I agree that this is an important interview of @PalantirTech's CEO.

Matches what I've heard from enterprise leaders.

Love what he is saying about AI, we don't need to overhype AI. It's hugely important without any hype, the most important set of new technologies of my life. By far. AI will affect every human on earth, in both positive and negative ways, and we no longer need to hype up that importance. 

He lays out the pressure that both military and enterprise is under and lays out the competition from China too. 

For me, this stuff is way over my pay grade, but why I track what's happening at the big dog feeding trough affects everyone down stream. 

Tomorrow I am headed to #acl2026. This is where AI engineers, researchers, developers are meeting to talk about arcane computer science work being done to make large language models better. 

It'll be interesting to see how those working deep in the bowels of all the companies Karp mentioned here, or at academic labs, are building around all these pressures up above. All the sessions are way deeper on technology than I typically go, so will be interesting to see what I learn over the next week and how I can translate it into something that's sharable here on X. 

Either way, Karp sure has a quick tongue which is refreshing to hear on CNBC. 

Definitely a must watch for anyone in AI.

items.title:
OpenAI CEO Sam Altman, in the Financial Times:

"Democratic institutions must not cede their respons...
items.content:
OpenAI CEO Sam Altman, in the Financial Times:

"Democratic institutions must not cede their responsibilities to AI labs. The labs develop the technology, but citizens and their elected representatives must make the rules."

Also via the FT: OpenAI has reportedly floated giving the US government a 5% stake in the company.

At OpenAI's $852B valuation, that's roughly $43B in equity.

The proposal goes beyond OpenAI: every major US lab (possibly Anthropic, Google, and Meta) would put up the same 5%. The FT says it's unclear whether any would agree.

Where the equity would go: a fund modeled on the Alaska Permanent Fund, which turns the state's oil money into dividend checks for Alaska's government and residents.

items.title:
My agent is now paying for its own data. No API key. No account. No credit card.

Check the video I ...
items.content:
My agent is now paying for its own data. No API key. No account. No credit card.

Check the video I recorded.

This uses the x402 open protocol built by Coinbase, along with a simple skill. This is an open protocol governed by the Linux Foundation.

To complete a goal, an agent can now search for an actor on the Apify Store, pay for it, and use it without any human intervention.

The way it works is pretty simple:

1. Your agent finds an Actor it wants to use
2. It sends a request
3. The Actor sends back an HTTP 402 "Payment Required" response
4. Agent authorizes payment from a wallet in USDC on Base
5. The Actor runs
6. User receives the result

The model is pay-as-you-go: the agent authorizes a spending ceiling, pays only for usage, and the remaining balance is automatically settled.

This brings an entire marketplace of AI tools into the agentic economy.

Here is the skill you need. Add it to your agent, and it will take care of everything for you:

https://t.co/XOUav6W9aM

If you want to know how this works, including what you can do with $1, read this post:

https://t.co/Uy60PgoePf

Thanks to the Apify team for partnering with me on this post.

items.title: 3 years ago I gave a talk at the first @aiDotEngineer conference on "Advanced RAG" techniques in ord...
items.content:
3 years ago I gave a talk at the first @aiDotEngineer conference on "Advanced RAG" techniques in order to work around the limitations of naive RAG.

It's insane how much the world has changed since then, and the world has evolved into standardized, higher-level abstractions around agent harnesses and context.

Some general patterns:
1. Retrieval complexity can be encoded at the agent layer. This means that you can give relatively simple but performant search tools to an agent (e.g. really fast bm25, vector search), and let the agent reasoning enter the right queries to find the right results. 

2. To some extent this is still evolving, but I do think we will increasingly care less about "hacking" the context window and more about deciding what business context is relevant in the first place.

3. The way we build agents has fundamentally changed from defining code, to defining runbooks, to defining goals.

Big congrats to @swyx and the entire AI Engineer team for continuing to put out awesome conferences every year.
items.author: @jerryjliu0
items.summary: Jerry Liu reflects that three years after his Advanced RAG talk at the first AIE, agent building has evolved toward standardized abstractions, arguing retrieval complexity can shift to the agent layer, focus is moving from context hacking to selecting relevant business context, and agent building has moved from code to runbooks to goals.
items.reasoning: Thoughtful, experience-grounded perspective from the LlamaIndex founder on how RAG and agent architectures have matured, with good engagement and genuine insight.
items.summary_html: Jerry Liu reflects that three years after his Advanced RAG talk at the first AIE, agent building has evolved toward standardized abstractions, arguing retrieval complexity can shift to the agent layer, focus is moving from context hacking to selecting relevant business context, and agent building has moved from code to runbooks to goals.

It's insane how much the world has changed since then, and the world has evolved into standardized, higher-level abstractions around agent harnesses and context.

Some general patterns:

1. Retrieval complexity can be encoded at the agent layer. This means that you can give relatively simple but performant search tools to an agent (e.g. really fast bm25, vector search), and let the agent reasoning enter the right queries to find the right results.

2. To some extent this is still evolving, but I do think we will increasingly care less about "hacking" the context window and more about deciding what business context is relevant in the first place.

3. The way we build agents has fundamentally changed from defining code, to defining runbooks, to defining goals.

items.title:
I've improved the NotebookLM script on my news site: https://t.co/8L5xphk0qQ 

The pattern?

Have my...
items.content:
I've improved the NotebookLM script on my news site: https://t.co/8L5xphk0qQ 

The pattern?

Have my AI agent grab all posts from the AI community here on X via the X API. About 30,000 every day. Costs about $150 a day. This is why lists on X are so important (I have the most complete lists of the tech community here on X by far, and they are all public so you can build your own systems like this).

Then my AI agent, built by @blevlabs and me, analyzes all of them, builds the website above, and it writes a script for NotebookLM, which largely is the same as the essay you see on the top of that page.

At the bottom of the page is a "copy script" button for NotebookLM users. You can copy the script, paste it into NotebookLM, which then can build you a podcast (which I like a lot better than the videos it generates, which I include here from today's script).

It updates three times a day, around 8 a.m., noon, and 6 p.m. 

If major news is breaking I update it more often. 

Why do I call this a pattern? Because you'll see this pattern used a lot more to build personalized news systems. 

Next on the priority list? Get my newsletter to work and send out to the thousands who have subscribed (thank you for being patient). 

Unfortunately @beehiiv hasn't turned on automatic posting yet, so am looking at other email systems that can be automated, like @resend. 

Eventually I want to turn this into an automatically generated news show with @HeyGen. 

Thanks for putting up with my agent that has been spitting out bad links lately, Brayden and I have been working on that behind the scenes to make the system more reliable. 

What do you think about the news site? I built it because it is just impossible for anyone to read the 30,000 posts a day that the AI industry generates here on X. 

Hope it helps you keep up with the models, papers, robots, company news, events, and more that everyone posts here, but that X itself has made it hard to find due to an inferior search system and an algorithm that only brings a certain kind of post to your ForYou feed, which hides a TON of interesting stuff being shared here.

My lists at https://t.co/9eRY65x3IQ are getting a LOT more complete and better, been working hard on those and watch them all day long because the quality of the lists (and completeness) makes this whole pattern better.

items.title:
And it's not locked to DeepSeek's checkpoints. 🧩

The Speculators library (https://t.co/pLpZP4xqfA) ...
items.content:
And it's not locked to DeepSeek's checkpoints. 🧩

The Speculators library (https://t.co/pLpZP4xqfA) lets you train and package DSpark draft models in a standard, HF-compatible format that vLLM loads directly. Already validated on Qwen3-8B and GLM-5.2. Run it on vLLM nightly now:

vllm serve zai-org/GLM-5.2-FP8 -tp 8 --speculative-config '{"method":"dspark","model":"RedHatAI/GLM-5.2-speculator.dspark-preview","num_speculative_tokens":7,"attention_backend":"FLASH_ATTN","draft_sample_method":"greedy"}'

speculators support PR: https://t.co/ZIuOMe7zP1

Thanks to the @RedHat_AI team for the Speculators integration! 🙏
items.author: @vllm_project
items.summary: The vLLM project explains its Speculators library lets developers train and package DSpark draft models in an HF-compatible format loaded directly by vLLM, already validated on Qwen3-8B and GLM-5.2, with a serving command example.

Special episode with @bentannyhill  on the Max Agency podcast. A month ago, his team shipped LangSmith Engine, our agent that hunts through your agent's failures, prioritizes issues, and drafts the fix.

We dive into the architecture decisions -- from how we used sandboxes to how we created subagents. And we also discuss an interesting challenge -- how we were able to build evals for an agent that never stops running.

Check out the full conversation ⤵️

⏯️ YouTube: https://t.co/2yEjgUNUu4

🎧 Apple: https://t.co/V69kYf7wTm

🟢 Spotify: https://t.co/lQWG9UMNVl

items.title:
Your agent is probably just a workflow, and one 603-page paper proves it.

A workflow runs the steps...
items.content:
Your agent is probably just a workflow, and one 603-page paper proves it.

A workflow runs the steps you wrote but an agent decides its own next step.

The Hitchhiker's Guide to Agentic AI, a paper by Haggai Roitman, maps that whole gap. It treats an agent as a model inside a system, not a clever prompt.

The core idea is a loop: observe, reason, act, then stop or ask for help. Everything else is the stack you build around it to keep it safe.

It walks the full stack:
> retrieval and memory systems
> the harness and orchestration
> tools, MCP, and A2A
> multi-agent coordination and evaluation

Most tasks never need all of it. That shows when an agent is worth it.

The honest takeaway: start simple, add autonomy only when the job demands it.

items.title: Full prompt: you have unity and access to an MCP server. I want you to build a game that is a unique...
items.content:
Full prompt: you have unity and access to an MCP server. I want you to build a game that is a unique twist on a FPS, with excellent graphics and such (download assets as needed). First generate 20 unique ideas, then figure out how to make the top 5 better, then pick  one. you want the player to say "wow" and "so clever" and also to enjoy the core gameplay loopp. make this great.

Then I asked "cool, how do i make the game playable for others? Can we use Netlify?"

It suggested WebGL, and then I had to click a couple permissions and that was it

items.title:
Your data may be wrong, outdated, manipulated, or incomplete.

Trusting it without continuous verifi...
items.content:
Your data may be wrong, outdated, manipulated, or incomplete.

Trusting it without continuous verification can turn business intelligence into business risk.

Modern organizations cannot assume that data is reliable just because it is stored in a system.

Microblog by @antgrasso

Data integrity needs continuous checks, real-time anomaly monitoring, and clear visibility across processes.

Access to critical systems must be controlled because weak identity rules can expose sensitive information or allow unauthorized changes.

Sources and data flows also need validation. When teams do not know where data comes from, how it moves, or who can modify it, trust becomes fragile.

Cybersecurity and information integrity are no longer only IT responsibilities. They are operational requirements for every team that uses data to make decisions.
items.author: @antgrasso
items.summary: Ant Grasso microblog arguing that data can be wrong or manipulated and that trusting it without continuous verification turns business intelligence into risk, calling for real-time anomaly monitoring and access control.
items.reasoning: Coherent data-integrity and cybersecurity argument from a known influencer with solid retweet engagement, relevant to enterprise AI trustworthiness.

Silicon Valley’s new battle: which robot has the best hands.

Why I am getting around to the robot companies. 

The guy who builds the hands for @1x_tech’s Neo gave me a tour of its headquarters.

I hear theirs, that will come out of stealth soon, are pretty good. And that their World Model is better than anyone other than Tesla.

I have a list of everyone in robotics, both here and in China if you want. 

Much engineering effort is being spent.

items.title: In FlexOlmo, each module is the size of a full model, so the combined system grows fast as more get ...
items.content:
In FlexOlmo, each module is the size of a full model, so the combined system grows fast as more get added.

FlexMoRE replaces most modules with compact representations. Its best config matches or beats FlexOlmo using less than one-third the parameters. https://t.co/fb5ghcIK63

items.summary: Allen AI explains FlexMoRE replaces full-size modules with compact representations, matching or beating FlexOlmo using under a third of the parameters.
items.reasoning: Concrete quantitative research claim on parameter efficiency in modular models.

items.title:
I'm not just studying world models just for robotics; as important as that is.

Someday they will ru...
items.content:
I'm not just studying world models just for robotics; as important as that is.

Someday they will run trillions of dollars of human potential.

They have deep implications for entertainment as well. It's always been my dream to have a holodeck, at least since I saw one on Star Trek, and every day such a thing becomes more and more possible. 

In 18 months, it's going to be able to simulate anything your mind can come up with, allowing you to:
1. Walk around said thing
2. Manipulate said thing
3. Interact with said thing
4. Talk to said thing
5. Play with said thing
6. Create with said thing (among other things)

You will want to watch the video I will have up in the morning from one of the top AI research labs in the world in Silicon Valley.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer describes studying world models beyond robotics, envisioning a holodeck-like system within 18 months that lets users walk around, manipulate and interact with simulated things.
items.reasoning: Speculative but articulate vision of world models for entertainment with decent engagement, though heavily forward-looking and promotional.
items.summary_html: Scobleizer describes studying world models beyond robotics, envisioning a holodeck-like system within 18 months that lets users walk around, manipulate and interact with simulated things.
items.content_html:
I'm not just studying world models just for robotics; as important as that is.

Someday they will run trillions of dollars of human potential.

They have deep implications for entertainment as well. It's always been my dream to have a holodeck, at least since I saw one on Star Trek, and every day such a thing becomes more and more possible.

In 18 months, it's going to be able to simulate anything your mind can come up with, allowing you to:

1. Walk around said thing

2. Manipulate said thing

3. Interact with said thing

4. Talk to said thing

5. Play with said thing

6. Create with said thing (among other things)

You will want to watch the video I will have up in the morning from one of the top AI research labs in the world in Silicon Valley.

Latest example, Alex Karp came out advocating for American open-source models as a necessity! At the same time, @PalantirTech is a free org on HF with 0 open-source models and 0 public datasets shared.

Time to switch from talking to contributing for all!

items.title: The reason these robot hands are important to pay attention to is because they are the first ones I ...
items.content:
The reason these robot hands are important to pay attention to is because they are the first ones I have seen that can actually pick up 35 pounds. 

Consider this use case: when you are going to the airport, you are going to want your robot to pick up your 55-pound suitcase and put it in the back of the Cybercab. These two hands could do that. 

They are very dexterous and very resilient to injury, which makes them worth watching. Thank you.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scoble explains why the robot hands matter: they can lift 35 pounds, illustrated with an airport suitcase and Cybercab use case.
items.reasoning: Concrete capability detail on dexterous robot hands with decent engagement, adding substance to the robotics thread.

items.title: You really need your own benchmarks. If you are translating hieroglyphics, use Gemini 3.5 Flash. If ...
items.content:
You really need your own benchmarks. If you are translating hieroglyphics, use Gemini 3.5 Flash. If you are running a vending machine use Opus 4.8. 

(This is one reason why I am skeptical of just swapping out models to optimize costs or generic benchmarks without testing first)

items.content:
/2 NotebookLM is turning source material into short visual lessons.

Google is rolling out Short Video Overviews, a new format that creates ~60-second vertical videos from uploaded or referenced sources.

It is built for quick learning:
> Explains key concepts
> Highlights major takeaways
> Uses narrative + educational animations
> Runs on Nano Banana 2 Lite

Useful before studying, reviewing research, or digging into long documents.

items.title:
The question with AI is, how much value are you getting out of a token? 

If you could make a millio...
items.content:
The question with AI is, how much value are you getting out of a token? 

If you could make a million dollars with every single token you developed, you would want to generate as many tokens as possible. But if you're not getting anything out of generating a token, spending money on it is a folly.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer restates his token-value framing of AI economics as a standalone post.
items.reasoning: Same token-value point reaching wider audience (13k views) but repetitive with no new depth.
items.summary_html: Scobleizer restates his token-value framing of AI economics as a standalone post.
items.content_html:
The question with AI is, how much value are you getting out of a token?

If you could make a million dollars with every single token you developed, you would want to generate as many tokens as possible. But if you're not getting anything out of generating a token, spending money on it is a folly.

items.title:
@cryptopunk7213 The question with AI is, how much value are you getting out of a token? 

If you cou...
items.content:
@cryptopunk7213 The question with AI is, how much value are you getting out of a token? 

If you could make a million dollars with every single token you developed, you would want to generate as many tokens as possible. But if you're not getting anything out of generating a token, spending money on it is a folly.
items.author: @Scobleizer

If you could make a million dollars with every single token you developed, you would want to generate as many tokens as possible. But if you're not getting anything out of generating a token, spending money on it is a folly.

Enterprise AI challenges are not always about model accuracy.
In many operational environments, the issue is timing.
 
The factory has already made the defect. The robot already moved. The process already drifted. When response times lag behind operations, even strong AI systems can lose impact.
 
A thread on latency, edge AI, and operational responsiveness.
 
@TMobileBusiness Partner

items.title: @IMissMyKid420 @mchlhess Actually, I do, and I can bring my real estate agent to talk about it if yo...
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer defends his account of Silicon Valley real estate bidding wars, linking rising prices to billions pouring into the AI industry.
items.reasoning: Connects AI investment to local housing pressure, an interesting side effect, but mostly a personal argument with low engagement.
items.summary_html: Scobleizer defends his account of Silicon Valley real estate bidding wars, linking rising prices to billions pouring into the AI industry.
items.content_html:
@IMissMyKid420 @mchlhess Actually, I do, and I can bring my real estate agent to talk about it if you want. She has been a family friend for three decades, and this happens on a regular basis: a client waits outside while the real estate agent goes in and negotiates over price with other agents, essentially running an auction.

This is why housing prices in Silicon Valley have gone up so much over the years. The pressure is always on the upside because there is not enough property here to buy and there is significant demand. Between tech companies moving a lot of people here and the AI industry getting really hot, the market is stretched thin.

If you haven't seen the multi-billions of dollars being invested in AI, you aren't paying attention and you certainly aren't thinking about where that money goes. There is simply not enough inventory to supply the billions of dollars pouring into the economy here in Silicon Valley and San Francisco.

items.title:
Everyone is scaling AI…
But almost no one is fixing the one thing that can create operational challe...
items.content:
Everyone is scaling AI…
But almost no one is fixing the one thing that can create operational challenges.

It’s not your model.
It’s not your data.
It’s your connectivity infrastructure.

When connectivity is disrupted, AI operations can impact operational visibility and connected workflows. I’ve been exploring this with @TMobileBusiness - and it’s a challenge more organizations are evaluating.

They’ve introduced SuperBroadband: An integrated connectivity solution that combines T-Mobile’s 5G network and Starlink to help businesses support resilient operations across remote and hard-to-reach environments.

Here’s why resilient connectivity is becoming increasingly important for AI-driven operations…

T-Mobile for Business Partner #SuperBroadband

items.title: /3 Loop engineering is how developers make AI agents build better software through repeated feedback...
items.content:
/3 Loop engineering is how developers make AI agents build better software through repeated feedback.

The idea is built around three loops:

> Agentic coding loop: AI writes, tests, and improves code.
> Developer feedback loop: humans steer product direction and UI decisions.
> External feedback loop: users, testers, and data shape what gets built next.

The lesson is simple: better agents do not remove humans.

They make human context more important. 

https://t.co/atME4TFZfG
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignal explains loop engineering, where agentic coding, developer feedback, and external feedback loops combine, arguing better agents increase the importance of human context.
items.reasoning: A reasonable conceptual framing of agentic development, but it is repackaged educational content with negligible engagement.

items.title: @c_s_a_w @JayLiStanford @proceptionAI We cover that in the video. That's job number one: to keep the...
items.content:
@c_s_a_w @JayLiStanford @proceptionAI We cover that in the video. That's job number one: to keep these hands reliable.

There are a lot of steps to proving that, and he hasn't started manufacturing them at scale yet. That will be the real challenge:
1. Can he make a thousand or a hundred thousand for customers?
2. Can they stand up in real use?

They are doing as much engineering as I have ever seen a hands company do. They even pick up a 35-pound barbell and remain reliable, so I think they have figured a lot of things out.
items.author: @Scobleizer

items.reasoning: Adds nuance about scaling and reliability, but low engagement.

items.content_html:
@c_s_a_w @JayLiStanford @proceptionAI We cover that in the video. That's job number one: to keep these hands reliable.

There are a lot of steps to proving that, and he hasn't started manufacturing them at scale yet. That will be the real challenge:

1. Can he make a thousand or a hundred thousand for customers?

2. Can they stand up in real use?

They are doing as much engineering as I have ever seen a hands company do. They even pick up a 35-pound barbell and remain reliable, so I think they have figured a lot of things out.

The courses cover b...
items.content:
/5 UC Berkeley has two free courses for learning LLM agents from the ground up.

The courses cover both foundational and advanced agent concepts, including:

> Reasoning and planning
> Tool use and RAG
> Agent infrastructure
> Multi-agent collaboration
> ... and more

The instructors and guest lecturers include people from UC Berkeley, Google DeepMind, Meta, and other top AI labs.

A strong starting point if you want to understand how agents actually work. 

https://t.co/5DpJFJH0xG

As businesses scale AI-enabled operations, resilient connectivity infrastructure is be...
items.content:
The takeaway:
As businesses scale AI-enabled operations, resilient connectivity infrastructure is becoming increasingly important.

AI-enabled operations increasingly depend on resilient network infrastructure.

Solutions like SuperBroadband are designed around an integrated connectivity approach that combines T-Mobile’s 5G network and Starlink connectivity to support resilient operations across demanding environments.

I break this down in the latest video.

Curious—how are you approaching resilient connectivity for AI at scale?

Learn More: https://t.co/EfeaMwa5Sz 

@TMobileBusiness Partner

Here’s what that looks like in practice:
 
Manufacturing → AI identifies a defect, but the response loop is delayed → More flawed products continue down the line → Waste and rework increase
Logistics → Robots and workflows wait for updated instructions → Small delays compound across operations
 
Industrial environments → Sensors identify anomalies, but delayed responses can affect operational continuity
 
The challenge is not always the model itself. Sometimes it’s the timing of the response.

Here’s what that looks like in practice:

Logistics → Robots and workflows wait for updated instructions → Small delays compound across operations

Industrial environments → Sensors identify anomalies, but delayed responses can affect operational continuity

The challenge is not always the model itself. Sometimes it’s the timing of the response.

For years, the focus was: Can AI do this?
 
Now the better question is: Can AI respond fast enough to influence what happens next?
 
Models are improving quickly. Access to AI is expanding.
 
That means operational responsiveness is becoming more important across manufacturing, logistics, and industrial environments.

items.title:
The shift I’m seeing with leading companies is simple:
They’re treating connectivity like critical i...
items.content:
The shift I’m seeing with leading companies is simple:
They’re treating connectivity like critical infrastructure.

The model:
→ Multiple connectivity technologies
→ Working together to support connected operations

Example:
→ T Mobile’s 5G network
→ Starlink connectivity

Designed to support resilient connectivity across locations and environments.

That’s the idea behind SuperBroadband—an integrated connectivity approach designed to support resilient operations.

items.title:
I see this pattern everywhere.

Companies invest heavily in:

→ Models
→ Data platforms
→ Automation...
items.content:
I see this pattern everywhere.

Companies invest heavily in:

→ Models
→ Data platforms
→ Automation
 
But resilient connectivity is still overlooked.

When connectivity becomes inconsistent, operational visibility and connected workflows can become more difficult to manage.

AI didn’t fail.

The underlying infrastructure became a challenge.
That’s not just an IT issue—it’s an operational resilience issue.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Van Loon sponsored post arguing companies overlook resilient connectivity even while investing in models and data, framing it as operational resilience not just IT.
items.reasoning: Marketing-driven infrastructure argument with minimal engagement.

items.content_html:
I see this pattern everywhere.

Companies invest heavily in:

→ Models

→ Data platforms

→ Automation

But resilient connectivity is still overlooked.

When connectivity becomes inconsistent, operational visibility and connected workflows can become more difficult to manage.

AI didn’t fail.

The underlying infrastructure became a challenge.

That’s not just an IT issue—it’s an operational resilience issue.

It’s to support decision-making closer to where data is generated.

Teams can also manage deployments through T-Platform, T-Mobile’s unified dashboard for advanced network solutions.

items.title: @17straight_17 @mchlhess I am not a bot, and this actually happened to me. There have been bidding w...
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer continues defending his Silicon Valley housing bidding-war anecdote against accusations of being a bot.
items.reasoning: Personal argument with minimal AI relevance and negligible engagement.
items.summary_html: Scobleizer continues defending his Silicon Valley housing bidding-war anecdote against accusations of being a bot.
items.content_html:
@17straight_17 @mchlhess I am not a bot, and this actually happened to me. There have been bidding wars over Silicon Valley property for decades.

I’ve bought multiple houses here, and one of our family friends is a real estate agent. This happens on a regular basis: the client sits out in the street while the real estate agent goes into the house to negotiate over price. Sometimes the price can go up hundreds of thousands of dollars over asking because it is a hot property, and most properties here are hot.

I have watched the for sale signs in the valley and they rarely last more than a few weeks, which tells me that things are still very hot and very contested. Finding a house near San Francisco right now is very difficult, and if you don't understand that, you don't live here.

@01PASHA501 @JayLiStanford @proceptionAI Instead of just throwing barbs, why don't you add some value?

Tell us what looks better in the hand space in robotics to you, and why.

These are very well-designed and can pick up 35 pounds, which I haven't seen others do yet. That's interesting.

Going to the airport, your suitcase will be 55 pounds. But if the robot has two hands, it can handle that weight and put it in a car. That's a big deal.

items.title:
Paris looks like a postcard.

I think the city is the most beautiful among all the Western European ...
items.content:
Paris looks like a postcard.

I think the city is the most beautiful among all the Western European capitals. It's big, colorful, walkable, charming, and full of life wherever you go.

Every time I come, I feel an immense joy that is hard to explain.

I don't speak French, but I've never had issues communicating with locals. I know Americans say that French people are rude, but I think it's just a difference in how we interact with others.

The food is second to none. In my book, it's one of the top 3 cuisines, along with Italian and Spanish food.

One of the things that impresses me most is the city's design.

Around 1850, Napoleon III decided to redesign Paris and appointed Baron Georges-Eugène Haussmann to do it.

The guy didn't pull any punches.

He demolished a large part of the city, created massive boulevards, built parks throughout Paris, modernized the city’s water and sewer systems, and created avenues connecting its most important monuments.

But the most important thing he did was establish building regulations.

Thanks to this, Paris still looks like a postcard: buildings of similar height, with a shared architectural style.

No matter where you look, you see the same elegant apartment buildings, with iron balconies and soft cream-colored façades.

Beautiful.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: Santiago writes an extended appreciation of Paris including its Haussmann redesign.
items.reasoning: Off-topic travel essay with no AI content despite decent reach.
items.summary_html: Santiago writes an extended appreciation of Paris including its Haussmann redesign.
items.content_html:

One of the things that impresses me most is the city's design.

The guy didn't pull any punches.

But the most important thing he did was establish building regulations.

No matter where you look, you see the same elegant apartment buildings, with iron balconies and soft cream-colored façades.
